category.label: Strategies
category.description: Strategy archetypes the literature has identified again and again, plus a complete asset-class catalogue drawn from Kakushadze & Serur (2018) and the broader literature: mean reversion, trend following, pairs / stat-arb, volatility, portfolio construction, FX (carry, dollar carry, momentum-carry combo, triangular arb), fixed income (curve positioning, immunization, butterflies, curve spreads, factor investing, CDS basis and swap-spread arbitrage), and commodities (roll yield, hedging pressure, skewness, value, momentum). Each entry includes the original empirical evidence and the in-app screen to prototype it.

entries.title: Strategy Patterns — the Catalog
entries.subtitle: Five recurring archetypes — mean reversion, trend / momentum, carry / yield, volatility, statistical arbitrage — what each one earns, what it dies on, and where to prototype it.

external_links.title: Kakushadze & Serur — 151 Trading Strategies

entries.title: Mean Reversion (Single-Name)
entries.subtitle: Buying drawdowns, selling rips. Z-score thresholds, Ornstein-Uhlenbeck calibration, half-life estimation, and why short-horizon mean reversion mostly works in equities (and mostly doesn't in FX majors).

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — mean reversion template

external_links.title: Lo & MacKinlay (1990) — When are contrarian profits due to overreaction?

entries.title: Trend Following (TSMOM)
entries.subtitle: Time-series momentum across asset classes. The classic 12-1 lookback, signed-vol scaling, the equity-bond-FX-commodity universe of TSMOM, and why long-only equity trend rarely works on its own.

external_links.title: Moskowitz, Ooi, Pedersen (2012) — Time-Series Momentum
Hurst, Ooi, Pedersen — A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following Investing

entries.title: Pairs Trading and Cointegration
entries.subtitle: Build a stationary spread, fade the deviations, exit at mean. Engle-Granger and Johansen tests, half-life rules, the Gatev-Goetzmann-Rouwenhorst evidence, and why pairs profits decayed after 2003.

external_links.title: Gatev, Goetzmann, Rouwenhorst — Pairs Trading: Performance of a Relative-Value Arbitrage Rule
Engle & Granger (1987) — Cointegration and Error Correction

entries.title: Statistical Arbitrage (Cross-Sectional)
entries.subtitle: Hundreds of legs, sector- and beta-neutral. The Avellaneda-Lee residual mean-reversion model, factor-residual construction, and the realities of capacity and crowding.

external_links.title: Avellaneda & Lee (2010) — Statistical Arbitrage in the US Equities Market

entries.title: Volatility Trading and the Surface
entries.subtitle: Implied vs realized, term structure, volatility risk premium, variance swaps, and the strategies you can build off the volatility surface.

external_links.title: Sinclair — Volatility Trading (2e)

entries.title: Portfolio Construction (Markowitz, Risk Parity, Kelly)
entries.subtitle: Mean-variance with shrinkage, equal-risk-contribution / risk parity, and Kelly-style growth-optimal weights — how to combine alpha streams without giving back the alpha.

external_links.title: Markowitz (1952) — Portfolio Selection
Asness, Frazzini, Pedersen — Leverage Aversion and Risk Parity

entries.title: FX Carry Trade
entries.subtitle: Borrow low-rate, invest high-rate. The forward premium puzzle (Fama 1984), the HML-Carry construction (Lustig-Roussanov-Verdelhan 2011), and the crash-risk story (Brunnermeier-Nagel-Pedersen 2008) — why the trade pays a premium and why it occasionally gives years back in months.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — FX carry template

external_links.title: Lustig, Roussanov, Verdelhan (2011) — Common Risk Factors in Currency Markets
Brunnermeier, Nagel, Pedersen (2008) — Carry Trades and Currency Crashes
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §8.2

entries.title: Dollar Carry Trade
entries.subtitle: A directional USD bet driven by the average forward discount across foreign currencies (Lustig-Roussanov-Verdelhan 2014). Long all foreign forwards when foreign rates exceed US rates on average; short when the reverse. A time-series carry signal that complements the cross-sectional HML-carry.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — dollar carry template

external_links.title: Lustig, Roussanov, Verdelhan (2014) — Countercyclical Currency Risk Premia
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §8.3

entries.title: FX Momentum + Carry Combo
entries.subtitle: Minimum-variance blending of FX carry and FX trend signals. Two roughly uncorrelated return streams combined into one book — the two-asset Markowitz solution applied to strategy returns. The crash months of carry are partially offset by trend's defensive positioning.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — FX combo template

external_links.title: Asness, Moskowitz, Pedersen (2013) — Value and Momentum Everywhere
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §8.4

entries.title: FX Triangular Arbitrage
entries.subtitle: The textbook arbitrage: if A→B→C→A loop produces more than you started, take the gap. In practice an HFT trade in major pairs (median opportunity lifetime < 1 second per Akram-Rime-Sarno 2008); a slower, real trade in EM crosses. Mostly useful to understand why FX spreads are tight.

external_links.title: Akram, Rime, Sarno (2008) — Arbitrage in the FX Market
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §8.5

entries.title: Yield Curve Positioning — Bullets, Barbells, Ladders, Rolling Down
entries.subtitle: Four directional bond-portfolio structures across maturities. Bullets concentrate at one point; barbells maximise convexity at a yield cost; ladders smooth reinvestment risk; rolling-down captures the carry from aging bonds on a steep upward-sloping curve. Same instruments, very different curve views.

external_links.title: Grantier (1988) — Convexity and Bond Performance
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §5.2-5.4, §5.12

entries.title: Bond Immunization
entries.subtitle: Match a bond portfolio's duration (and ideally convexity) to a future liability's horizon so that parallel rate shifts hurt assets and liabilities equally. Redington (1952), Fisher-Weil (1971), and the foundation of liability-driven investing (LDI) for pensions and insurers.

external_links.title: Redington (1952) — Review of the Principles of Life Office Valuations
Fisher & Weil (1971) — Coping with the Risk of Interest-Rate Fluctuations
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §5.5

entries.title: Bond Butterflies — Curvature Trades
entries.subtitle: Three-maturity duration-neutral positions (long wings / short body, or vice versa) that isolate the curvature of the yield curve. Four variants: dollar-duration-neutral, fifty-fifty, regression-weighted, maturity-weighted. PC3 of the bond-return decomposition.

external_links.title: Grieves (1999) — Butterfly Trades
Litterman & Scheinkman (1991) — Common Factors Affecting Bond Returns
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §5.6-5.8

entries.title: Yield Curve Spreads — Flatteners and Steepeners
entries.subtitle: Directional bets on the slope of the curve. Long 2s10s steepener when expecting Fed cuts; short the spread when expecting hikes. PC2 of bond returns, the watched recession indicator (Estrella-Mishkin), and the canonical macro-rates trade.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — curve spread template

external_links.title: Estrella & Hardouvelis (1991) — The Term Structure as a Predictor of Real Economic Activity
Estrella & Mishkin (1998) — Predicting U.S. Recessions
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §5.13

entries.title: Bond Factor Investing — Low-Risk, Value, Carry
entries.subtitle: Equity-style factor investing in corporate and Treasury bonds. Low-risk anomaly (short-duration, high-rated bonds outperform on risk-adjusted basis), value (bonds with wider-than-fair-modelled spreads), and carry (yield plus roll-down). Houweling-van Vundert (2017) is the synthesis.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — bond factor template

external_links.title: Houweling & van Vundert (2017) — Factor Investing in the Corporate Bond Market
Frazzini & Pedersen (2014) — Betting Against Beta
Koijen, Moskowitz, Pedersen, Vrugt (2018) — Carry
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §5.9-5.11

entries.title: Credit Arbitrage — CDS Basis and Swap-Spread
entries.subtitle: Two classic fixed-income 'arbitrages' that are really compensation for funding risk. CDS basis arb (bond + protection = synthetic risk-free) and swap-spread arb (swap rate vs Treasury yield financed at repo). Both made huge profits — until 2008 broke the financing.

seen_in.label: Backtesting — fixed-income arb with funding shocks

external_links.title: Choudhry (2007) — Trading the CDS Basis
Duarte, Longstaff, Yu (2006) — Risk and Return in Fixed-Income Arbitrage
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §5.14-5.15

entries.title: Commodity Roll Yield
entries.subtitle: The unique-to-commodities return source: rolling long positions in backwardated curves earns positive yield, rolling in contango bleeds. Keynes's normal backwardation, the theory of storage, and the cross-sectional long-backwardation / short-contango premium (Erb-Harvey 2006, Gorton-Rouwenhorst 2006).

external_links.title: Erb & Harvey (2006) — The Strategic and Tactical Value of Commodity Futures
Gorton & Rouwenhorst (2006) — Facts and Fantasies About Commodity Futures
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §9.1

entries.title: Commodity Hedging Pressure
entries.subtitle: Use weekly CFTC Commitments of Traders (COT) data to identify commodities where producers are heavily short (paying speculators a premium per Keynes). Long the producer-hedged backwardation candidates, short the consumer-hedged contango candidates. Basu-Miffre 2013, de Roon-Nijman-Veld 2000.

external_links.title: Basu & Miffre (2013) — Capturing the Risk Premium of Commodity Futures: The Role of Hedging Pressure
de Roon, Nijman, Veld (2000) — Hedging Pressure Effects in Futures Markets
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §9.2

entries.title: Commodity Skewness Premium
entries.subtitle: Long negatively-skewed commodities, short positively-skewed ones (Fernandez-Perez et al. 2018). Investors over-pay for 'lottery-ticket' positive-skew commodities (Barberis-Huang 2008) and under-pay for negatively-skewed ones — the premium is the gap.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — skewness factor template

external_links.title: Fernandez-Perez et al. (2018) — The Skewness of Commodity Futures Returns
Barberis & Huang (2008) — Stocks as Lotteries
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §9.5

entries.title: Commodity Value and Momentum
entries.subtitle: The two factors that work in every asset class — applied to commodities. Value uses 5-year price ratios (long-horizon mean reversion); momentum uses 12-1 trailing returns. Asness-Moskowitz-Pedersen 2013 and Miffre-Rallis 2007 are the canonical references. Roughly uncorrelated, additive Sharpe in combination.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — commodity multi-factor template

external_links.title: Asness, Moskowitz, Pedersen (2013) — Value and Momentum Everywhere
Miffre & Rallis (2007) — Momentum Strategies in Commodity Futures Markets
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §9.4

entries.title: Stock Price Momentum (Jegadeesh-Titman)
entries.subtitle: Buy past winners, sell past losers. The 12-1 cross-sectional momentum factor (Jegadeesh-Titman 1993), the Carhart UMD factor, the 2009 momentum crash, and how vol-scaling (Barroso-Santa-Clara 2014) and quality overlays (AFP 2019) keep the factor tradeable.

external_links.title: Jegadeesh & Titman (1993) — Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers
Daniel & Moskowitz (2016) — Momentum Crashes
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.1

entries.title: Earnings Momentum (PEAD / SUE)
entries.subtitle: Post-earnings-announcement drift: companies that surprise positively on EPS continue to outperform for weeks. SUE-based long-short construction (Chan-Jegadeesh-Lakonishok 1996), Bernard-Thomas 1989 underreaction story, and the slow decay since the 2000s.

external_links.title: Bernard & Thomas (1989) — Post-Earnings-Announcement Drift
Chan, Jegadeesh, Lakonishok (1996) — Momentum Strategies
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.2

entries.title: Value Factor (Book-to-Price)
entries.subtitle: Cheap stocks (high B/P) outperform expensive stocks (low B/P) on risk-adjusted basis. Fama-French (1992/1993), the value-vs-growth 2007-2020 drawdown, the intangibles question (Arnott et al 2021), and the 2022 revival.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — value factor template

external_links.title: Fama & French (1992) — The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns
Arnott, Harvey, Kalesnik, Linnainmaa (2021) — Reports of Value's Death May Be Greatly Exaggerated
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.3

entries.title: Low-Volatility Anomaly (BAB)
entries.subtitle: Low-beta and low-vol stocks earn higher risk-adjusted returns than high-vol stocks. Betting Against Beta (Frazzini-Pedersen 2014), leverage aversion, lottery preference (Bali-Brown-Cakici), and benchmark-as-limit-to-arbitrage (Baker-Bradley-Wurgler 2011).

external_links.title: Frazzini & Pedersen (2014) — Betting Against Beta
Ang, Hodrick, Xing, Zhang (2006) — The Cross-Section of Volatility and Expected Returns
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.4

entries.title: Implied Volatility Signal (Cross-Sectional)
entries.subtitle: Stocks with rising call IV outperform; stocks with rising put IV underperform. Informed-trading-leaks-from-options story (Cremers-Weinbaum 2010, An-Ang-Bali-Cakici 2014). Useful complement in multi-factor books, decayed since the 2010s.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — IV signal template

external_links.title: An, Ang, Bali, Cakici (2014) — The Joint Cross Section of Stocks and Options
Cremers & Weinbaum (2010) — Deviations from Put-Call Parity
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.5

entries.title: Multifactor Equity Portfolio
entries.subtitle: Combine value, momentum, low-vol, quality, profitability into a single book. Integrated-score vs. portfolio-blending methods (Asness-Frazzini-Pedersen). Why factor diversification raises Sharpe and the limits of factor timing.

external_links.title: Asness, Moskowitz, Pedersen (2013) — Value and Momentum Everywhere
Asness, Frazzini, Pedersen (2019) — Quality Minus Junk
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.6, §3.20

entries.title: Residual Momentum
entries.subtitle: Apply momentum to FF3 regression residuals instead of raw returns. Same expected return, half the volatility — roughly double the Sharpe (Blitz-Huij-Martens 2011). Largely immune to the 2009 momentum crash because it has no factor-beta tilt.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — residual momentum template

external_links.title: Blitz, Huij, Martens (2011) — Residual Momentum
Daniel & Moskowitz (2016) — Momentum Crashes
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.7

entries.title: Technical Analysis — MA, Support/Resistance, Channels
entries.subtitle: Moving-average crossovers, pivot-based support/resistance, Donchian channels. Honest framing: TA is contested at single-stock daily horizons but underlies trend-following systems. Brock-Lakonishok-LeBaron (1992), Lo-Mamaysky-Wang (2000), Park-Irwin (2007) survey.

external_links.title: Brock, Lakonishok, LeBaron (1992) — Simple Technical Trading Rules
Lo, Mamaysky, Wang (2000) — Foundations of Technical Analysis
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.11-3.15

entries.title: Merger (Risk) Arbitrage
entries.subtitle: Capture the spread between announced M&A offer price and target trading price. Cash deals (long target) and stock deals (long target / short acquirer). Mitchell-Pulvino 2001 documented Sharpe ~0.8 in normal times, short-put-like exposure during crises (2008).

external_links.title: Mitchell & Pulvino (2001) — Characteristics of Risk and Return in Risk Arbitrage
Baker & Savaşoglu (2002) — Limited Arbitrage in Mergers and Acquisitions
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.16

entries.title: Market-Making and Alpha Combos (HFT)
entries.subtitle: Quote both sides of the order book to capture spreads. Adverse selection is the central problem (Glosten-Milgrom 1985, Kyle 1985). Modern HFT modulates quoting with short-horizon directional signals. Alpha combos (Kakushadze-Yu 2017) combine hundreds of weak signals into a tradeable mega-alpha.

external_links.title: Glosten & Milgrom (1985) — Bid, Ask and Transaction Prices
Kyle (1985) — Continuous Auctions and Insider Trading
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §3.19-3.20

entries.title: ETF Sector Rotation
entries.subtitle: Buy outperforming sector ETFs, optionally filter with moving averages and add a broad-market trend gate (dual momentum, Antonacci 2014). Moskowitz-Grinblatt 1999 showed industry momentum drives much of stock momentum — the ETF version captures it with 9 trades instead of 3000.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — sector rotation template

external_links.title: Moskowitz & Grinblatt (1999) — Do Industries Explain Momentum?
Faber (2007) — A Quantitative Approach to Tactical Asset Allocation
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §4.1-4.3

entries.title: ETF Mean Reversion (Internal Bar Strength)
entries.subtitle: IBS = (close − low) / (high − low). Long ETFs with IBS near 0 (weak close), short those with IBS near 1 (strong close). Pagonidis 2014 documented Sharpe 1.5-2.5 gross on country ETFs — captures end-of-day flow dislocations that mean-revert overnight.

external_links.title: Levy & Lieberman (2013) — Overreaction of Country ETFs to US Market Returns
Marshall, Nguyen, Visaltanachoti (2013) — ETF Arbitrage: Intraday Evidence
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §4.4

entries.title: Leveraged ETF Decay (Short Both Sides)
entries.subtitle: LETFs systematically decay against their stated multiple due to compounded volatility. Short both leveraged-long and leveraged-short ETFs on the same underlying to capture decay regardless of direction. Avellaneda-Zhang 2010 formalised the math. Pays steadily in choppy markets, hurts in trending markets — and the 2018 XIV liquidation is the cautionary tale.

external_links.title: Avellaneda & Zhang (2010) — Path-Dependence of Leveraged ETF Returns
Charupat & Miu (2011) — The Pricing and Performance of Leveraged Exchange-Traded Funds
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §4.5

entries.title: Multi-Asset Trend Following on ETFs
entries.subtitle: Trend following across equity, bond, REIT, commodity, currency ETFs. The ETF version of Moskowitz-Ooi-Pedersen TSMOM and Hurst-Ooi-Pedersen century-of-evidence — same Sharpe ~0.7-1.0 with one-tenth the operational complexity of futures. 2008 was its best year; 2022 reminded everyone the strategy still works.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — multi-asset trend template

external_links.title: Moskowitz, Ooi, Pedersen (2012) — Time Series Momentum
Hurst, Ooi, Pedersen (2017) — A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following Investing
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §4.6

entries.title: Index Cash-and-Carry Arbitrage
entries.subtitle: Theoretical no-arbitrage: index futures price = spot − dividends + carry. When the actual futures price diverges, an HFT-style arbitrage locks in profit. MacKinlay-Ramaswamy 1988 documented S&P futures mispricings. Modern opportunities are sub-second. Also covers intraday SPY/IVV ETF arbitrage (§6.4, Marshall et al 2013).

external_links.title: Cornell & French (1983) — The Pricing of Stock Index Futures
MacKinlay & Ramaswamy (1988) — Index-Futures Arbitrage
Marshall, Nguyen, Visaltanachoti (2013) — ETF Arbitrage: Intraday Evidence
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §6.2, §6.4

entries.title: Dispersion Trading
entries.subtitle: Long single-stock straddles, short an index straddle. Captures the correlation risk premium (Driessen-Maenhout-Vilkov 2009) — implied index vol is typically higher than weighted single-stock vol because of institutional put demand (Gârleanu-Pedersen-Poteshman 2009). The 2008 cautionary tale: when correlations spike to 1, the trade loses spectacularly.

external_links.title: Driessen, Maenhout, Vilkov (2009) — The Price of Correlation Risk
Gârleanu, Pedersen, Poteshman (2009) — Demand-Based Option Pricing
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §6.3

entries.title: Index Volatility Targeting
entries.subtitle: Scale risky-asset exposure inversely to realised volatility (w = σ*/σ). Equity vol is negatively correlated with returns (Moreira-Muir 2017) — the strategy improves Sharpe and substantially reduces drawdowns. Harvey-Hoyle et al 2018 documented the effect across 60+ markets.

external_links.title: Moreira & Muir (2017) — Volatility-Managed Portfolios
Harvey et al (2018) — The Impact of Volatility Targeting
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §6.5

entries.title: VIX Futures Basis Trading
entries.subtitle: The VIX futures basis (UX1 vs spot VIX) has substantial forecasting power for subsequent futures price changes (Simon-Campasano 2014, Mixon 2007). Sell UX1 in deep contango, buy in deep backwardation — hedged with mini-S&P to neutralise the VIX-equity beta. The Feb 2018 Volmageddon is the tail-risk warning.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — VIX basis template

external_links.title: Simon & Campasano (2014) — The VIX Futures Basis
Whaley (2000) — The Investor Fear Gauge
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §7.2

entries.title: VIX ETN Decay Trade (Short VXX / Long VXZ)
entries.subtitle: VXX (front-month VIX ETN) systematically loses to roll in contango. Short VXX, hedge with VXZ (mid-maturity VIX ETN) or a VIX futures basket. Avellaneda-Papanicolaou 2018 formalised the math. 2010-2017 was a printing press; February 2018 (Volmageddon) wiped out XIV in one day — the cautionary tale.

external_links.title: Avellaneda & Papanicolaou (2018) — Statistics of VIX Futures and Trading Volatility ETPs
Bordonado, Molnár, Samdal (2017) — VIX Exchange Traded Products
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §7.3

entries.title: Volatility Skew — Long Risk Reversal
entries.subtitle: OTM puts trade at higher IV than equidistant OTM calls (the equity vol skew). Long risk reversal (buy OTM call, sell OTM put) captures this skew premium with bullish delta. Bates 1991 documented the post-1987 origin; Bollen-Whaley 2004 on net buying pressure. The trade is bullish + short skew + short vega.

external_links.title: Bollen & Whaley (2004) — Net Buying Pressure and Implied Vol Functions
Bates (1991) — The Crash of '87
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §7.5

entries.title: Hedging Risk with Futures
entries.subtitle: Direct hedge (matching futures contract), cross-hedge (proxy contract — jet fuel via heating oil), and interest-rate hedging (Treasury futures for bond portfolios). Ederington 1979 optimal hedge ratio. The Metallgesellschaft 1993 and LTCM 1998 lessons on maturity mismatch and funding-liquidity risk.

external_links.title: Ederington (1979) — The Hedging Performance of the New Futures Markets
Ankirchner et al (2012) — Futures Cross-Hedging with a Stationary Basis
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §10.1

entries.title: Futures Calendar Spread
entries.subtitle: Long one expiry, short another of the same underlying — isolates the term structure. Bull spread (long near / short deferred) bets on tight supply; bear spread bets on glut. Natural gas March-April is the canonical seasonal spread; crude oil March 2020 contango blowout is the cautionary tale.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — calendar spread template

external_links.title: Bessembinder (1992) — Systematic Risk, Hedging Pressure, and Risk Premiums in Futures Markets
Adrangi et al (2006) — Petroleum Spreads and the Term Structure of Futures Prices
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §10.2

entries.title: Futures Contrarian (Mean-Reversion)
entries.subtitle: Cross-sectional mean reversion across futures contracts: long underperformers, short outperformers w.r.t. weekly market index. Bessembinder et al 1995 documented mean reversion in the futures term structure. Volume + open-interest filter (Bianchi-Drew-Fan 2015) improves the signal.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — futures contrarian template

external_links.title: Bessembinder, Coughenour, Seguin, Smoller (1995) — Mean Reversion in Equilibrium Asset Prices
Bianchi, Drew, Fan (2015) — Combining Momentum with Reversal in Commodity Futures
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §10.3

entries.title: Fundamental Macro Momentum
entries.subtitle: Rank countries on 4 macro state variables (business cycle, trade, monetary policy, risk sentiment), construct cross-asset long-short across equities, bonds, currencies. Brooks 2017 (AQR) documented Sharpe 0.7-0.9 over 50+ years and 30+ markets. The macro-level cousin of price momentum.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — macro momentum template

external_links.title: Brooks (2017) — A Half Century of Macro Momentum
Asness, Moskowitz, Pedersen (2013) — Value and Momentum Everywhere
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §19.2

entries.title: Global Macro Inflation Hedge
entries.subtitle: Use the headline-vs-core inflation spread as a leading indicator: when HI > CI, an oil/food shock is in progress that hasn't yet flowed to core. Scale commodity allocation accordingly (Fulli-Lemaire 2013 formula). Worked spectacularly in 2021-2022; the canonical macro inflation hedging strategy.

seen_in.label: AI Quant Lab — inflation hedge template

external_links.title: Fulli-Lemaire (2013) — An Inflation Hedging Strategy with Commodities
Gorton & Rouwenhorst (2006) — Facts and Fantasies About Commodity Futures
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §19.3

entries.title: Trading on Economic Announcements (Pre-FOMC Drift)
entries.subtitle: Lucca-Moench 2015: ~80% of US equity risk premium was earned on the 8 FOMC meeting days per year. Be 100% in equities on announcement days, 100% in Treasuries otherwise. Effect weakened post-publication but still tradeable. Cieslak-Morse-Vissing-Jorgensen 2019 updated the analysis.

external_links.title: Lucca & Moench (2015) — The Pre-FOMC Announcement Drift
Savor & Wilson (2013) — How Much Do Investors Care About Macroeconomic Risk?
Cieslak, Morse, Vissing-Jorgensen (2019) — Stock Returns over the FOMC Cycle
Kakushadze & Serur (2018) — 151 Trading Strategies, §19.5
